narrative: As a Black male applicant with a high family income background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and an LSAT score of 29, both below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students in our dataset. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failing at 40.6%—a risk level high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed right away. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, which strongly signaled potential struggles on the bar exam compared to higher-performing students. On a more positive note, your high family income level worked significantly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success by suggesting access to resources that often help students prepare effectively. Your undergraduate GPA also contributed positively, as it was close to average and indicated a solid academic foundation despite not being exceptional. These top three elements—your Year 3 performance as the biggest concern, offset somewhat by family support and prior GPA—together tipped the scales against admission. While full-time enrollment is the norm and didn't sway things much, your lower LSAT and Year 1 decile of 1 reinforced the overall cautious outlook. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law; it's the model weighing patterns from thousands of past students where early and mid-performance like yours correlated with bar challenges. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your grades in any remaining coursework to climb those deciles, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and family-backed preparation. Many applicants in similar situations reapply after targeted improvements, turning predictions around—your high family income positions you well for test prep or additional undergrad-level courses if needed. With focused effort, you can reshape this story for future applications.
